title: Sample Shelf
desc: The dockside workroom is full of lake samples and glittering residue that need order before the damp turns them to clutter.

steps.text:
Najarin's lower workroom is built almost at water level, and the lake never lets the place forget it. Light comes in through high windows in wavering bands. Damp gathers on the stone by morning, then dries into a faint mineral glitter by afternoon. Shelves of sealed water jars line one wall; shallow sediment trays rest on the benches under clean cloths. Nothing here is grand, but everything in the room is part of the castle's patient habit of looking closely at wonder instead of merely admiring it.

A stack of fresh labels has been left beside the ledgers. The work is straightforward: sort and relabel the sample jars, or rinse and restack the sediment trays before the residue cakes hard.

options.label: Sort and relabel the sample jars on the shelf
Rinse and restack the sediment trays on the bench

steps.text:
The jars differ in the ways that matter here: depth taken, shoreline point, mineral haze, tunnel proximity, and whether the water inside catches stray light with ordinary sparkle or something calmer and stranger. You sort them shelf by shelf, replace the tags that damp has blurred, and return the rows in clear order so the whole wall reads from shallow to deep, from open shore to watched tunnel.

As the light shifts, every relabeled bottle holds a different version of the lake: pale blue, silver, a line of castle stone, a shard of sky. By the time you finish, the shelf no longer looks like clutter rescued from damp. It looks like a quiet archive of the water outside.

options.label: Hang the spare tags to dry and leave the shelves in order

steps.text:
The sediment trays are meant to dry flat so every grain and crystalline fleck can be compared later. Yesterday's damp left them sticking and dull. You rinse them clean, scrape the residue channels clear, and rebuild the stack with proper spacers so air can move between the trays. Freed from the film, the remaining grains catch the window light in soft flashes of white, gold, and faint green.

Set out in order again, the trays look unexpectedly beautiful, as if a little piece of the lakebed has learned how to glitter without moving. The room stays silent except for water against the dock stones below. At Ken-I-Po, even the evidence of wonder asks to be kept carefully.

options.label: Set the last tray in place and leave the bench clear
